topic.what: Investigation of the gastroprotective effects of Emblica officinalis (Amla) fruit extract in rat models of ethanol- and aspirin-induced gastric ulceration, with mechanistic focus on antioxidant and cytoprotective pathways.
topic.how: In-vivo rodent study using ethanol-induced and aspirin-induced gastric ulcer models; measured ulcer index, gastric acid secretion, pepsin activity, mucus content, and antioxidant enzyme levels (SOD, catalase, GSH) in gastric mucosa.
topic.why_matters: Amla is the central ingredient in Triphala and a foundational Ayurvedic rasayana herb; demonstrating its gastroprotective mechanism in standard animal models provides pharmacological validation for its classical use in pitta-aggravating conditions affecting the stomach.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Macroscopic gastric lesion score after ethanol challenge
characteristics.evidence: Statistically significant lesion score reduction in ethanol model reported in the study
characteristics.context: Rat model; ethanol dose-induced acute gastric damage

characteristics.what_it_measures: Mucosal mucus glycoprotein levels as surrogate for mucosal barrier integrity
characteristics.value: Elevated mucus content in Amla-treated group
characteristics.evidence: Biochemical mucus assay in gastric tissue
characteristics.context: Single-dose acute model; chronic dosing effects not assessed

characteristics.value: Significantly preserved enzyme activities vs. ulcerated controls
characteristics.evidence: Enzymatic assays on gastric tissue homogenates
characteristics.context: Rodent gastric tissue; human mucosal antioxidant pharmacokinetics unknown

characteristics.evidence: Titrimetric acid measurement in gastric contents
characteristics.context: Effect magnitude small; primary mechanism appears cytoprotective rather than antisecretory

good_for: Mechanistic grounding of Amla gastroprotection for Ayurvedic formulation rationale
Identifying antioxidant cytoprotection as a distinct gastroprotective mechanism beyond acid suppression
Supporting inclusion of Amla in Triphala and other polyherbal gastric formulas
Bridging classical pitta-cooling concept to mucosal antioxidant defense biology
not_for: H. pylori eradication — no antimicrobial endpoint studied
Clinical dosing guidance — animal-to-human translation not established
Chronic gastritis models — acute ulcer model only
requires: Human pharmacokinetic data for tannins and vitamin C from Amla
Validation in H. pylori-associated or NSAID-induced chronic gastritis models
Dose standardisation relative to active constituents (gallic acid, ascorbic acid)

novelty: Early mechanistic validation (2002) demonstrating Amla cytoprotection operates via antioxidant enzyme preservation rather than acid suppression alone
Provides experimental basis for the traditional Ayurvedic use of Amla as a mucosal tonic in pitta disorders
Distinguishes Amla's mechanism from classical antacid or antisecretory herbs, positioning it as a mucosal defense enhancer
open_problems: No chronic gastritis animal model or clinical trial data
Active constituent responsible for gastroprotection not isolated (tannins vs. vitamin C vs. saponins)
Interaction with H. pylori colonisation not studied
Bioavailability of key phytochemicals after oral administration not addressed
